Introduction: In the Registry Editor, users can navigate through various registry keys to view and modify settings. One common location is HKEY_USERS\.DEFAULT\Software\, which contains configuration data for the default user profile on the system.

Windows system registry: HKEY_USERS\.DEFAULT\Software\
Introduction: Users should be cautious when modifying the registry, as incorrect changes can cause system instability or even prevent the system from booting. It is recommended to back up the registry before making changes and to only make modifications if you are familiar with the potential consequences.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Control\SafeBoot\
Introduction:  This registry key is related to the Safe Mode boot option in Windows. Safe Mode is a diagnostic mode of the Windows operating system that only loads essential system files and drivers, allowing for troubleshooting of issues that may occur during normal boot. The key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Control\SafeBoot\ is where the settings for Safe Mode boot options are stored.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Control\SafeBoot\
Introduction: Within this key, you may find subkeys corresponding to different types of Safe Mode boot options, such as Minimal and Network. These subkeys contain information about the drivers and services that are allowed to load in Safe Mode. Making changes to these settings can affect how Windows boots into Safe Mode and which components are loaded during boot.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Control\SafeBoot\
Introduction: It is important to be cautious when making changes to the Windows registry, as incorrect modifications can cause system instability and potentially prevent Windows from booting properly. It is recommended to backup the registry before making any changes and to only make changes if you are familiar with the registry and its impact on your system.

Introduction: This registry key contains information about the installation properties of a specific product with the unique identifier "0E9201899CF73FC4BA93F631631229A1" under the UserData key for the user with the security identifier S-1-5-18. The InstallProperties key may contain data such as the product name, version, installation location, and other properties related to the installation of the product. These values are used by the Windows Installer service to manage installed programs on the system.

Windows system registry: HKEY_USERS\

Windows system registry: HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\
Introduction: Explorer\Shell Folders

Windows system registry: HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\
Introduction: This registry key contains information about the default locations for various shell folders in Windows, such as the Desktop, Start Menu, and Program Files. Users can also customize these locations by changing the values in this registry key.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\
Introduction: It is important to be cautious when making changes to the Windows system registry, as incorrect modifications can cause issues with the operating system. It is recommended to create a backup of the registry before making any changes.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\Interface\{E9A5593C-CAB0-11D1-8C0B-0000F8754DA1}\
Introduction: This registry key contains information about interfaces in the Windows operating system. The specific interface {E9A5593C-CAB0-11D1-8C0B-0000F8754DA1} may correspond to a specific software component or system function. It is important to note that modifying the Windows registry can have serious consequences for system stability and functionality, so it is recommended to exercise caution when making changes to registry keys.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\hkSFV.SFV.File\
Introduction: This registry key stores information related to the file type hkSFV.SFV.File. The HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT key contains information about file associations and class identifiers for different file types on a Windows system. The hkSFV.SFV.File key may contain subkeys and values that define how the system should handle files of this type, including which program should be used to open them and what actions should be taken when they are accessed. Modifying or deleting entries in this registry key can affect the behavior of files with the hkSFV.SFV.File extension. It is important to be cautious when making changes to the Windows system registry, as incorrect modifications can cause system errors or instability.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\Folder\shellex\ContextMenuHandlers\
Introduction: Here you can find the context menu handlers for folders in the Windows system registry. These handlers are responsible for adding options to the right-click menu when you click on a folder in File Explorer. Each subkey under this path represents a different context menu handler that can add custom actions or options to the folder's context menu.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\Interface\{2C787A50-E01C-11CF-8E74-00A0C90F26F8}\
Introduction: This registry key is related to a specific interface in Windows operating system. It contains information about the interface with the Class ID {2C787A50-E01C-11CF-8E74-00A0C90F26F8}. This interface may be used by applications or services to interact with this specific class in the Windows system. The information stored in this registry key helps Windows system to properly register and manage the interface and its interactions with other components. Making changes to this registry key without proper knowledge or understanding can potentially cause issues with the Windows system.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\TypeLib\{C5981376-3B25-4F7E-9EE0-E89E4DEB2B22}\
Introduction: This registry key contains information about a specific Type Library ({C5981376-3B25-4F7E-9EE0-E89E4DEB2B22}) registered on the system. Type libraries contain information about COM objects, interfaces, and data types that can be used by applications to interact with COM components. This particular key would hold information about the version, location, and other details of the specified Type Library.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\.ace\
Introduction: This registry key contains information about the file association and properties for files with the extension ".ace". The HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT hive contains settings that are global to all users on the computer, and file associations are typically stored here to ensure consistency across all user profiles. The values under this key determine which program will be used to open files with the .ace extension, as well as any additional options or settings associated with those files.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\.ace\
Introduction: Modifying values in this registry key should be done carefully, as incorrect changes can potentially cause issues with file associations and system functionality. It is always recommended to backup the registry before making any changes.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Classes\Search.EmbeddedGatherNotify\
Introduction: This registry key is related to embedded search functionality within Windows. It is responsible for gathering notifications related to embedded searches on the system. It is part of the Windows system registry where various settings and configurations related to Search functionality are stored. Modifying this key without proper knowledge or understanding can potentially cause problems with the search functionality on the system. It is advisable to proceed with caution when dealing with system registry keys and make backups before making any changes.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\Applications\
Introduction: The HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\Applications\ registry key contains information about applications installed on the system. This includes file associations, program capabilities, and other settings related to applications. Developers can use this registry key to register their applications and specify default behaviors for file types.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\vcredist_x86.exe\
Introduction: This registry key is related to the Image File Execution Options feature in Windows. It allows for specific configurations to be set for individual executable files. In this case, the key is specifically for the "vcredist_x86.exe" file, which is related to the Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable package for 32-bit systems.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\vcredist_x86.exe\
Introduction: By modifying the settings in this registry key, you can control how the specified executable file behaves when it is executed. This can be useful for troubleshooting or debugging purposes.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\iriverplus3.File.Video\
Introduction: This registry key contains the settings and configuration information for the iriverplus3 application related to video files. It includes information such as file type associations, shell commands, and other settings specific to video files within the iriverplus3 program. Making changes to these settings can affect how the iriverplus3 application handles video files on your Windows system.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Keyboard\Native Media Players\
Introduction: This registry key contains settings related to native media players on the system. This may include configurations for keyboard shortcuts or other settings specific to media players that are integrated with the Windows operating system. It is generally not recommended to make changes to the registry unless you are familiar with the implications and potential risks.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\MediaPlayer\Player\
Introduction: This registry key stores information specific to the Windows Media Player program for the current user. This can include settings, preferences, and other configurations related to media playback and management within the program. It is important not to make any changes to these registry keys unless you are familiar with the potential implications on the proper functioning of the program.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\Interface\{8556BCD2-E01E-11CF-8E74-00A0C90F26F8}\
Introduction: This registry key contains information about COM interfaces on the system. The key "{8556BCD2-E01E-11CF-8E74-00A0C90F26F8}" represents a specific interface in the system. The information stored in this key includes details about the interface, such as its name, properties, methods, and other relevant data. This information is used by the system and applications to interact with the interface and facilitate communication between different components. Changes to this key should be made with caution, as they can impact the functionality and stability of the system.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Enum\Root\MS_PSCHEDMP\0001\
Introduction: This registry key is related to the Microsoft Packet Scheduler Miniport driver. The Packet Scheduler is a component of the Windows operating system that provides quality of service (QoS) capabilities for network traffic.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Enum\Root\MS_PSCHEDMP\0001\
Introduction: Within this registry key, you may find settings and configurations related to the specific instance of the MS_PSCHEDMP driver identified by the "0001" subkey. This may include information about the driver's installation, configuration, and other parameters.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Enum\Root\MS_PSCHEDMP\0001\
Introduction: Modifying registry keys, especially those related to system drivers, should be done with caution as incorrect changes can lead to system instability or malfunctions. It is recommended to backup the registry before making any changes.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Classes\.odh\
Introduction: This registry key contains information about the file association for files with the .odh file extension. It may define what program is used to open files with the .odh extension and how those files are treated by the operating system.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\Interface\{648A5602-2C6E-101B-82B6-000000000014}\

Introduction: This subkey likely contains information related to an interface or component registered on the system. Interface keys in the registry often store information about COM (Component Object Model) interfaces that are used by applications and system components.

Introduction: Developers and software applications may use interface keys in the registry to define and manage interfaces for communication between different components or objects in a program.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\Interface\{648A5602-2C6E-101B-82B6-000000000014}\
Introduction: It is important to note that directly modifying the Windows registry can have serious consequences for system stability and performance. Making incorrect changes to registry keys and values can cause software malfunctions, system errors, or even render the system inoperable. Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Applian Technologies\
Introduction: This registry key stores settings and information related to software developed by Applian Technologies on a Windows system. Users can access and modify settings in this registry key using the Registry Editor tool in Windows. Windows system registry: HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\WinRAR\shellex\PropertySheetHandlers\
Introduction:  This registry key is used to store information related to WinRAR's property sheet handlers, which are used to modify the properties of WinRAR files and archives. Property sheet handlers allow users to view and modify metadata such as file properties, access permissions, and archival settings.

Windows system registry: registry
Introduction: A central hierarchical database used in Windows 98, Windows CE, Windows NT, and Windows 2000 used to store information that is necessary to configure the system for one or more users, applications, and hardware devices.

Windows system registry: registry
Introduction: The Registry contains information that Windows continually references during operation, such as profiles for each user, the applications installed on the computer and the types of documents that each can create, property sheet settings for folders and application icons, what hardware exists on the system, and the ports that are being used.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CURRENT_USER
Introduction: Contains the root of the configuration information for the user who is currently logged on. The user's folders, screen colors, and Control Panel settings are stored here. This information is associated with the user's profile. This key is sometimes abbreviated as HKCU.

Windows system registry: HKEY_USERS
Introduction: Contains all the actively loaded user profiles on the computer. HKEY_CURRENT_USER is a subkey of HKEY_USERS. HKEY_USERS is sometimes abbreviated as HKU.

Windows system registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE
Introduction: Contains configuration information particular to the computer (for any user). This key is sometimes abbreviated as HKLM.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT
Introduction: Is a subkey of HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software. The information that is stored here makes sure that the correct program opens when you open a file by using Windows Explorer. This key is sometimes abbreviated as HKCR. Starting with Windows 2000, this information is stored under both the HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE and HKEY_CURRENT_USER keys.

Windows system registry: HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG
Introduction: Contains information about the hardware profile that is used by the local computer at system startup.

Windows system registry: registry
Introduction: Each key has a name consisting of one or more printable characters. Key names are not case sensitive. Key names cannot include the backslash character (\), but any other printable character can be used. Value names and data can include the backslash character.

API: get system directory
Introduction: Retrieves the path of the system directory. The system directory contains system files such as dynamic-link libraries and drivers.This function is provided primarily for compatibility. Applications should store code in the Program Files folder and persistent data in the Application Data folder in the user's profile.

API: write console
Introduction: Writes a character string to a console screen buffer beginning at the current cursor location.

API: kernel open file
Introduction: The NtOpenFile routine opens an existing file, directory, device, or volume.

API: kernel create process
Introduction: The ZwOpenProcess routine opens a handle to a process object and sets the access rights to this object.Creates a new process and its primary thread. The new process runs in the security context of the calling process.If the calling process is impersonating another user, the new process uses the token for the calling process, not the impersonation token.

API: get system info
Introduction: Displays detailed configuration information about a computer and its operating system, including operating system configuration, security information, product ID, and hardware properties (such as RAM, disk space, and network cards).

API: write console
Introduction: Writes a character string to a console screen buffer beginning at the current cursor location.

API: kernel read virtual memory
Introduction: The NtAllocateVirtualMemory routine reserves, commits, or both, a region of pages within the user-mode virtual address space of a specified process.

API: remove directory
Introduction: Deletes an existing empty directory.

API: find first file
Introduction: Retrieves the status of the specified virtual key. The status specifies whether the key is up, down, or toggled (on, off—alternating each time the key is pressed).

API: kernel query key
Introduction: The ZwQueryKey routine provides information about the class of a registry key, and the number and sizes of its subkeys.

API: open service
Introduction: Opens an existing service.

API: enum windows
Introduction: Enumerates all top-level windows on the screen by passing the handle to each window, in turn, to an application-defined callback function. EnumWindows continues until the last top-level window is enumerated or the callback function returns FALSE.

API: virtual protect
Introduction: Changes the protection on a region of committed pages in the virtual address space of a specified process.

API: get volume name for volume mount point
Introduction: Retrieves a volume GUID path for the volume that is associated with the specified volume mount point ( drive letter, volume GUID path, or mounted folder).

API: http open request
Introduction: Creates an HTTP request handle.

API: http send request
Introduction: Sends the specified request to the HTTP server, allowing callers to send extra data beyond what is normally passed to HttpSendRequestEx.

API: get user name
Introduction: Retrieves the name of the user associated with the current thread.

API: rtl remove vectored exception handler
Introduction: Unregisters a vectored exception handler.

API: http open request
Introduction: Creates an HTTP request handle.

API: http send request
Introduction: Sends the specified request to the HTTP server, allowing callers to send extra data beyond what is normally passed to HttpSendRequestEx.

API: get system directory
Introduction: Retrieves the path of the system directory. The system directory contains system files such as dynamic-link libraries and drivers.

API: lookup privilege value
Introduction: The LookupPrivilegeValue function retrieves the locally unique identifier (LUID) used on a specified system to locally represent the specified privilege name.

API: read cabinet state
Introduction: [ReadCabinetState is available for use in the operating systems specified in the Requirements section. It may be altered or unavailable in subsequent versions.]

API: time get time
Introduction: The timeGetTime function retrieves the system time, in milliseconds. The system time is the time elapsed since Windows was started.

API: delete url cache entry
Introduction: Removes the file associated with the source name from the cache, if the file exists.

API: get disk free space
Introduction: Retrieves information about the amount of space that is available on a disk volume, which is the total amount of space, the total amount of free space, and the total amount of free space available to the user that is associated with the calling thread.

API: create remote thread
Introduction: Creates a thread that runs in the virtual address space of another process.Use the CreateRemoteThreadEx function to create a thread that runs in the virtual address space of another process and optionally specify extended attributes.

API: module32 first
Introduction: Retrieves information about the first module associated with a process.

API: net share enum
Introduction: Retrieves information about each shared resource on a server.You can also use the WNetEnumResource function to retrieve resource information. However, WNetEnumResource does not enumerate hidden shares or users connected to a share.

API: find window
Introduction: Retrieves a handle to a window whose class name and window name match the specified strings. The function searches child windows, beginning with the one following the specified child window. This function does not perform a case-sensitive search

API: kernel enumerate key
Introduction: The ZwEnumerateKey routine returns information about a subkey of an open registry key.

API: kernel free virtual memory
Introduction: The NtFreeVirtualMemory routine releases, decommits, or both releases and decommits, a region of pages within the virtual address space of a specified process.

API: reg query info key
Introduction: Retrieves information about the specified registry key.

API: create thread
Introduction: Creates a thread to execute within the virtual address space of the calling process.To create a thread that runs in the virtual address space of another process, use the CreateRemoteThread function.

API: open service
Introduction: Opens an existing service.

API: internet open
Introduction: Initializes an application's use of the WinINet functions.

API: open key
Introduction: The OpenKey routine opens an existing registry key.

API: cert create certificate context
Introduction: The CertCreateCertificateContext function creates a certificate context from an encoded certificate. The created context is not persisted to a certificate store. The function makes a copy of the encoded certificate within the created context.

API: ldr load dll
Introduction: Loads the specified module into the address space of the calling process. The specified module may cause other modules to be loaded.

API: get adapters addresses
Introduction: The GetAdaptersAddresses function retrieves the addresses associated with the adapters on the local computer.

API: find window
Introduction: Retrieves a handle to a window whose class name and window name match the specified strings. The function searches child windows, beginning with the one following the specified child window. This function does not perform a case-sensitive search.

API: control service
Introduction: Sends a control code to a service.

API: reg enum value
Introduction: Enumerates the values for the specified open registry key. The function copies one indexed value name and data block for the key each time it is called.

API: reg delete key
Introduction: Deletes a subkey and its values. Note that key names are not case sensitive.

API: kernel suspend thread
Introduction: Suspends the specified thread.

API: w s a socket
Introduction: The WSASocket function creates a socket that is bound to a specific transport-service provider.

API: internet open
Introduction: Initializes an application's use of the WinINet functions.

API: get user name
Introduction: Retrieves the name of the user or other security principal associated with the calling thread. You can specify the format of the returned name.

API: cert control store
Introduction: The CertControlStore function allows an application to be notified when there is a difference between the contents of a cached store in use and the contents of that store as it is persisted to storage. Differences can occur as another process makes a change that affects the store as it is persisted.

API: get user name
Introduction: Retrieves the name of the user or other security principal associated with the calling thread. You can specify the format of the returned name.

API: set end of file
Introduction: Sets the physical file size for the specified file to the current position of the file pointer.The physical file size is also referred to as the end of the file. The SetEndOfFile function can be used to truncate or extend a file. To set the logical end of a file, use the SetFileValidData function.

API: w s a socket
Introduction: The WSASocket function creates a socket that is bound to a specific transport-service provider.

API: reg enum value
Introduction: Enumerates the values for the specified open registry key. The function copies one indexed value name and data block for the key each time it is called.

API: reg delete key
Introduction: Deletes a subkey and its values. Note that key names are not case sensitive.

API: cert open store
Introduction: The CertOpenStore function opens a certificate store by using a specified store provider type. While this function can open a certificate store for most purposes, CertOpenSystemStore is recommended to open the most common certificate stores. CertOpenStore is required for more complex options and special cases.

API: get system time as file time
Introduction: Retrieves the current system date and time. The information is in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) format.

API: set value key
Introduction: The SetValueKey routine creates or replaces a registry key's value entry.

API: kernel device io control file
Introduction: Deprecated. Builds descriptors for the supplied buffer(s) and passes the untyped data to the device driver associated with the file handle.

API: process32 next
Introduction: Retrieves information about the next process recorded in a system snapshot.

API: uuid create
Introduction: The UuidCreate function creates a new UUID.

API: set file pointer
Introduction: This function stores the file pointer in two LONG values. To work with file pointers that are larger than a single LONG value, it is easier to use the SetFilePointerEx function.

API: create toolhelp32 snapshot
Introduction: Takes a snapshot of the specified processes, as well as the heaps, modules, and threads used by these processes.

API: find resource
Introduction: Determines the location of a resource with the specified type and name in the specified module.

API: ole initialize
Introduction: Initializes the COM library on the current apartment, identifies the concurrency model as single-thread apartment (STA), and enables additional functionality described in the Remarks section below. Applications must initialize the COM library before they can call COM library functions other than CoGetMalloc and memory allocation functions.

API: delete file
Introduction: Deletes an existing file.To perform this operation as a transacted operation, use the DeleteFileTransacted function.

API: kernel protect virtual memory
Introduction: Changes the protection on a region of committed pages in the virtual address space of the calling process.

API: crypt acquire context
Introduction: The CryptAcquireContext function is used to acquire a handle to a particular key container within a particular cryptographic service provider (CSP). This returned handle is used in calls to CryptoAPI functions that use the selected CSP.

API: create service
Introduction: Creates a service object and adds it to the specified service control manager database.

API: delete value key
Introduction: The DeleteValueKey routine deletes a value entry matching a name from an open key in the registry. If no such entry exists, an error is returned.

API: get async key state
Introduction: Determines whether a key is up or down at the time the function is called, and whether the key was pressed after a previous call to GetAsyncKeyState.

API: ldr get dll handle
Introduction: Retrieves a module handle for the specified module and increments the module's reference count unless GET_MODULE_HANDLE_EX_FLAG_UNCHANGED_REFCOUNT is specified. The module must have been loaded by the calling process.

API: rtl add vectored exception handler
Introduction: Registers a vectored exception handler.

API: select
Introduction: The select function determines the status of one or more sockets, waiting if necessary, to perform synchronous I/O.

API: shell execute
Introduction: Performs an operation on a specified file.

API: internet open url
Introduction: Opens a resource specified by a complete FTP or HTTP URL.

API: create service
Introduction: Creates a service object and adds it to the specified service control manager database.

API: set unhandled exception filter
Introduction: Enables an application to supersede the top-level exception handler of each thread of a process.After calling this function, if an exception occurs in a process that is not being debugged, and the exception makes it to the unhandled exception filter, that filter will call the exception filter function specified by the lpTopLevelExceptionFilter parameter.

API: is debugger present
Introduction: Determines whether the calling process is being debugged by a user-mode debugger.

API: load driver
Introduction: The LoadDriver routine loads a driver into the system.

API: find resource
Introduction: Determines the location of a resource with the specified type and name in the specified module.

API: get user name
Introduction: Retrieves the name of the user associated with the current thread.

API: connect
Introduction: The connect function establishes a connection to a specified socket.

API: module32 next
Introduction: Retrieves information about the next module associated with a process or thread.

API: get system windows directory
Introduction: Retrieves the path of the system directory. The system directory contains system files such as dynamic-link libraries and drivers.This function is provided primarily for compatibility. Applications should store code in the Program Files folder and persistent data in the Application Data folder in the user's profile.

API: dns query_
Introduction: The DnsQuery function type is the generic query interface to the DNS namespace, and provides application developers with a DNS query resolution interface. Like many DNS functions, the DnsQuery function type is implemented in multiple forms to facilitate different character encoding.

API: w s a recv from
Introduction: The WSARecvFrom function receives a datagram and stores the source address.

API: message box timeout
Introduction: Displays a modal dialog box that contains a system icon, a set of buttons, and a brief application-specific message, such as status or error information. The message box returns an integer value that indicates which button the user clicked.

API: crypt acquire context
Introduction: The CryptAcquireContext function is used to acquire a handle to a particular key container within a particular cryptographic service provider (CSP). This returned handle is used in calls to CryptoAPI functions that use the selected CSP.

API: message box timeout
Introduction: Displays a modal dialog box that contains a system icon, a set of buttons, and a brief application-specific message, such as status or error information. The message box returns an integer value that indicates which button the user clicked.

API: delay execution
Introduction: The KeDelayExecutionThread routine puts the current thread into an alertable or nonalertable wait state for a specified interval.

API: internet get connected state
Introduction: Retrieves the connected state of the local system.

API: reg enum key
Introduction: Enumerates the subkeys of the specified open registry key. The function retrieves information about one subkey each time it is called.

API: reg enum key
Introduction: Enumerates the subkeys of the specified open registry key. The function retrieves information about one subkey each time it is called.

API: get volume path name
Introduction: Retrieves the volume mount point where the specified path is mounted.

API: rtl decompress buffer
Introduction: The RtlDecompressBuffer function decompresses an entire compressed buffer.

API: crypt export key
Introduction: The CryptExportKey function exports a cryptographic key or a key pair from a cryptographic service provider (CSP) in a secure manner.A handle to the key to be exported is passed to the function, and the function returns a key BLOB. This key BLOB can be sent over a nonsecure transport or stored in a nonsecure storage location. This function can export an Schannel session key, regular session key, public key, or public/private key pair. The key BLOB to export is useless until the intended recipient uses the CryptImportKey function on it to import the key or key pair into a recipient's CSP.

API: sizeof resource
Introduction: Retrieves the size, in bytes, of the specified resource.

API: find first file
Introduction: Searches a directory for a file or subdirectory with a name and attributes that match those specified.

API: bind
Introduction: The bind function associates a local address with a socket.

Introduction: Retrieves the path of the shared Windows directory on a multi-user system.This function is provided primarily for compatibility. Applications should store code in the Program Files folder and persistent data in the Application Data folder in the user's profile.

API: reg create key
Introduction: Creates the specified registry key. If the key already exists, the function opens it. Note that key names are not case sensitive.

API: kernel query information file
Introduction: Before using a resource, an application must load it into memory. The FindResource and FindResourceEx functions find a resource in a module and return a handle to the binary resource data. FindResource locates a resource by type and name. FindResourceEx locates the resource by type, name, and language. Information about FindResource in this topic also applies to FindResourceEx.

API: kernel query information file
Introduction: The LoadResource function uses the resource handle returned by FindResource to load the resource into memory. After an application loads a resource by using LoadResource, the system will unload the associated memory only when all references to its module are freed through FreeLibrary. Applications which need to repeatedly access the same or many resources within a particular module may incur performance penalties due to the memory mapping taking place in repeated LoadLibrary and FreeLibrary calls. Applications should store a single module handle until resources are no longer needed, and then call FreeLibrary. After a module is unloaded from memory, resource handles become invalid.

API: listen
Introduction: The listen function places a socket in a state in which it is listening for an incoming connection.

API: get temp path
Introduction: Retrieves the path of the directory designated for temporary files.

API: find resource
Introduction: Determines the location of the resource with the specified type, name, and language in the specified module.

API: get file attributes
Introduction: Retrieves attributes for a specified file or directory.

API: internet set option
Introduction: Sets an Internet option.

API: reg delete value
Introduction: Deletes the subkeys and values of the specified key recursively.

Introduction: Retrieves the context of the specified thread.

Introduction: This header is used by System Services. For more information

API: kernel set information file
Introduction: The NtSetInformationFile routine changes various kinds of information about a file object.

API: get native system info
Introduction: Retrieves information about the current system to an application running under WOW64. If the function is called from a 64-bit application, it is equivalent to the GetSystemInfo function. If the function is called from an x86 or x64 application running on a 64-bit system that does not have an Intel64 or x64 processor (such as ARM64), it will return information as if the system is x86 only if x86 emulation is supported (or x64 if x64 emulation is also supported).

Introduction: Opens an File Transfer Protocol (FTP) or HTTP session for a given site.

API: process32 first
Introduction: Retrieves information about the first process encountered in a system snapshot.

API: co initialize
Introduction: Any Windows program that uses COM must initialize the COM library by calling the CoInitializeEx function. Each thread that uses a COM interface must make a separate call to this function.

API: kernel terminate process
Introduction: Terminates the specified process and all of its threads.

API: kernel enumerate value key
Introduction: The ZwEnumerateValueKey routine gets information about the value entries of an open key.

API: net get join information
Introduction: The NetGetJoinInformation function retrieves join status information for the specified computer.

API: getsockname
Introduction: The getsockname function retrieves the current name for the specified socket descriptor in name. It is used on the bound or connected socket specified by the s parameter. The local association is returned. This call is especially useful when a connect call has been made without doing a bind first; the getsockname function provides the only way to determine the local association that has been set by the system.

API: send
Introduction: The send function sends data on a connected socket.

API: kernel allocate virtual memory
Introduction: The NtAllocateVirtualMemory routine reserves, commits, or both, a region of pages within the user-mode virtual address space of a specified process.

API: gethostbyname
Introduction: The gethostbyname function returns a pointer to a hostent structure—a structure allocated by Windows Sockets. The hostent structure contains the results of a successful search for the host specified in the name parameter.

API: crypt decode object
Introduction: The CryptDecodeObjectEx function decodes a structure of the type indicated by the lpszStructType parameter.

API: get cursor pos
Introduction: Retrieves the position of the mouse cursor, in screen coordinates.

API: get file size
Introduction: Retrieves the size of the specified file.

API: find window
Introduction: Retrieves a handle to the top-level window whose class name and window name match the specified strings. This function does not search child windows. This function does not perform a case-sensitive search.

API: kernel write file
Introduction: The NtCreateFile routine creates a new file or opens an existing file.

API: cert open system store
Introduction: The CertOpenSystemStore function is a simplified function that opens the most common system certificate store. To open certificate stores with more complex requirements, such as file-based or memory-based stores, use CertOpenStore.

API: rtl create user thread
Introduction: Creates a thread to execute within the virtual address space of the calling process.

API: s h get folder path
Introduction: Deprecated. Gets the path of a folder identified by a CSIDL value.

Introduction: Releases, decommits, or releases and decommits a region of memory within the virtual address space of a specified process.

API: socket
Introduction: The socket function creates a socket that is bound to a specific transport service provider.

API: crypt encrypt
Introduction: The CryptEncrypt function encrypts data. The algorithm used to encrypt the data is designated by the key held by the CSP module and is referenced by the hKey parameter.

API: kernel set context thread
Introduction: Sets the context for the specified thread.

API: kernel open directory object
Introduction: [This function may be altered or unavailable in the future.]Opens an existing directory object.

API: kernel queue apc thread
Introduction: Adds a user-mode asynchronous procedure call (APC) object to the APC queue of the specified thread.

API: w s a startup
Introduction: The WSAStartup function must be the first Windows Sockets function called by an application or DLL. It allows an application or DLL to specify the version of Windows Sockets required and retrieve details of the specific Windows Sockets implementation. The application or DLL can only issue further Windows Sockets functions after successfully calling WSAStartup.

API: recv
Introduction: The recv function receives data from a connected socket or a bound connectionless socket.

API: getaddrinfo
Introduction: The getaddrinfo function provides protocol-independent translation from an ANSI host name to an address.

API: ldr get procedure address
Introduction: LdrGetProcedureAddress is an API for resolving function addresses dynamically. Collecting the function names given as an argument of the API, the method can collect the information on the function calls that were not intercepted in dynamic analysis.

API: internet query option
Introduction: Queries an Internet option on the specified handle.

API: internet crack url
Introduction: Cracks a URL into its component parts.

API: cert open system store
Introduction: The CertOpenSystemStore function is a simplified function that opens the most common system certificate store.

API: kernel read file
Introduction: Reads data from the specified file or input/output (I/O) device. Reads occur at the position specified by the file pointer if supported by the device.

API: co initialize security
Introduction: The CoInitializeSecurity function initializes the security layer and sets the specified values as the security default. If a process does not call CoInitializeSecurity, COM calls it automatically the first time an interface is marshaled or unmarshaled, registering the system default security. No default security packages are registered until then.

API: kernel query attributes file
Introduction: [This function may be altered or unavailable in the future.]Opens an existing directory object.

Introduction: Creates and default-initializes a single object of the class associated with a specified CLSID.

API: reg query value
Introduction: Retrieves the type and data for the specified value name associated with an open registry key.

API: thread32 first
Introduction: Retrieves information about the first thread of any process encountered in a system snapshot.

API: kernel create file
Introduction: Creates a new file or directory, or opens an existing file, device, directory, or volume.

API: crypt create hash
Introduction: The CryptCreateHash function initiates the hashing of a stream of data. It creates and returns to the calling application a handle to a cryptographic service provider (CSP) hash object. This handle is used in subsequent calls to CryptHashData and CryptHashSessionKey to hash session keys and other streams of data.

API: lookup account sid
Introduction: LookupAccountSidLocalW is defined as a macro that calls LookupAccountSidW with NULL as the first parameter. Retrieves the name of the account for the specified SID on the local machine.

API: kernel create mutant
Introduction: Creates or opens a named or unnamed mutex object.

API: setsockopt
Introduction: The setsockopt function sets the current value for a socket option associated with a socket of any type, in any state. Although options can exist at multiple protocol levels, they are always present at the uppermost socket level. Options affect socket operations, such as whether expedited data (OOB data for example) is received in the normal data stream, and whether broadcast messages can be sent on the socket.

API: get file size
Introduction: Retrieves the size of the specified file.

API: kernel resume thread
Introduction: Decrements a thread's suspend count. When the suspend count is decremented to zero, the execution of the thread is resumed.

API: s h get special folder location
Introduction: SHGetSpecialFolderLocation is not supported and may be altered or unavailable in the future. Instead, use SHGetFolderLocation.Retrieves a pointer to the ITEMIDLIST structure of a special folder.

API: internet crack url
Introduction: Cracks a URL into its component parts.

API: reg open key
Introduction: RegOpenKeyExopens a key in the Windows registry. The handle it gives must be used when you read to or write from any values under that key. Unlike RegCreateKeyEx, this function will not create the key if it does not exist. The function puts a handle to the opened key into the variable passed as phkResult.

API: ioctlsocket
Introduction: The ioctlsocket function controls the I/O mode of a socket.

API: send notify message
Introduction: Sends the specified message to a window or windows. If the window was created by the calling thread, SendNotifyMessage calls the window procedure for the window and does not return until the window procedure has processed the message. If the window was created by a different thread, SendNotifyMessage passes the message to the window procedure and returns immediately; it does not wait for the window procedure to finish processing the message.

API: crypt gen key
Introduction: The CryptGenKey function generates a random cryptographic session key or a public/private key pair. A handle to the key or key pair is returned in phKey. This handle can then be used as needed with any CryptoAPI function that requires a key handle.

API: delete service
Introduction: Marks the specified service for deletion from the service control manager database.

API: kernel query directory file
Introduction: NtQueryDirectoryFile is used to retrieve information about a directory. This function can be used for enumeration purposes. Library.

API: internet close handle
Introduction: Closes a single Internet handle.

API: w s a send
Introduction: The WSASend function is used to write outgoing data from one or more buffers on a connection-oriented socket specified by s. It can also be used, however, on connectionless sockets that have a stipulated default peer address established through the connect or WSAConnect function.

API: get file type
Introduction: Retrieves the file type of the specified file.

API: search path
Introduction: Searches for a specified file in a specified path.

API: reg query value
Introduction: Retrieves the type and data for the specified value name associated with an open registry key.

API: send notify message
Introduction: Sends the specified message to a window or windows. If the window was created by the calling thread, SendNotifyMessage calls the window procedure for the window and does not return until the window procedure has processed the message. If the window was created by a different thread, SendNotifyMessage passes the message to the window procedure and returns immediately; it does not wait for the window procedure to finish processing the message.

API: reg open key
Introduction: RegOpenKeyExopens a key in the Windows registry. The handle it gives must be used when you read to or write from any values under that key. Unlike RegCreateKeyEx, this function will not create the key if it does not exist. The function puts a handle to the opened key into the variable passed as phkResult.

API: crypt hash data
Introduction: The CryptHashData function adds data to a specified hash object. This function and CryptHashSessionKey can be called multiple times to compute the hash of long or discontinuous data streams.

API: get system metrics
Introduction: Retrieves the specified system metric or system configuration setting.

API: get disk free space
Introduction: Retrieves information about the specified disk, including the amount of free space on the disk.

API: kernel close
Introduction: NtClose is a generic routine that operates on any type of object. Closing an open object handle causes that handle to become invalid. The system also decrements the handle count for the object and checks whether the object can be deleted.

API: gethostbyname
Introduction: The following is a list of the reference content for the Windows application programming interface (API) for desktop and server applications.

API: get system directory
Introduction: 1. Privilege escalation: If an attacker is able to call the GetSystemDirectoryA API with elevated privileges, they may be able to gain unauthorized access to sensitive system files and directories.

API: get system directory
Introduction: 2. Information disclosure: By calling the GetSystemDirectoryA API, an attacker may be able to obtain information about the Windows system directory structure, which could be used to identify potential security vulnerabilities.

API: get system directory
Introduction: 3. Denial of service: An attacker could potentially use the GetSystemDirectoryA API to perform a denial of service attack by flooding the system with requests, causing the system to become unresponsive.

API: get system directory
Introduction: 4. Malware injection: By calling the GetSystemDirectoryA API, an attacker could potentially inject malware into the system directory, which could then be executed with System-level privileges.

API: get system directory
Introduction: 5. System integrity compromise: If an attacker is able to manipulate the output of the GetSystemDirectoryA API call, they may be able to compromise the integrity of the system by altering important system files and directories.

API: write console
Introduction: 1. Buffer Overflow: If improper input validation is performed, a malicious user could input a large amount of data, causing a buffer overflow, potentially leading to the execution of arbitrary code.

API: write console
Introduction: 2. Injection Attacks: Attackers could inject malicious code into the input fields, leading to the execution of unauthorized commands or actions on the system.

API: write console
Introduction: 3. Privilege Escalation: If the API is called with improper permissions, it could allow an attacker to escalate their privileges and gain unauthorized access to sensitive system resources.

API: write console
Introduction: 4. Denial of Service (DoS): An attacker could flood the API with excessive requests, causing the system to become overwhelmed and unresponsive, leading to a denial of service for legitimate users.

API: write console
Introduction: 5. Man-in-the-Middle Attacks: If the communication with the API is not properly encrypted, it could be intercepted by a malicious actor, allowing them to tamper with the data being sent or received.

API: write console

Introduction: 1. Injection attacks: Calling the Windows system API Module32FirstW can potentially open up opportunities for injection attacks by malicious actors. This API function is used to enumerate all loaded dynamic-link libraries (DLLs) in a specified process or all system-wide processes. If not properly handled, an attacker could inject malicious code into the application's memory space using this API.

API: module32 first
Introduction: 2. Information disclosure: Using Module32FirstW can inadvertently leak sensitive information about the system's processes and modules to unauthorized parties. This could be exploited by attackers to gather intelligence on the system's configuration and potentially aid in further targeted attacks.

API: module32 first
Introduction: 3. Privilege escalation: If Module32FirstW is not properly implemented with access controls and validation checks, it could potentially lead to privilege escalation attacks. An attacker could exploit vulnerabilities in the way the API function is used to gain elevated privileges and execute unauthorized commands on the system.

API: module32 first
Introduction: 4. Denial of Service (DoS) attacks: In scenarios where the Windows system API Module32FirstW is abused or used inefficiently, it could lead to resource exhaustion and result in a denial of service (DoS) attack. This could disrupt the normal operation of the system and impact its availability for legitimate users.

API: module32 first
Introduction: 5. Software vulnerabilities: Using Module32FirstW incorrectly or without proper error handling can introduce software vulnerabilities in the application. These vulnerabilities could be exploited by attackers to bypass security controls, gain unauthorized access, or compromise the integrity of the system. Regularly updating and patching the application is necessary to mitigate these risks.

Introduction: There are several security risks associated with calling the Windows system API NetShareEnum:

API: net share enum
Introduction: 1. Information disclosure: NetShareEnum retrieves a list of shared resources on a remote machine, including the names and paths of shared folders. If an attacker gains unauthorized access to this information, they may be able to gather sensitive data about the network and its users.

API: net share enum
Introduction: 2. Unauthorized access: Calling NetShareEnum may allow an attacker to gain access to shared resources on a remote machine that they should not have access to. This could lead to unauthorized data theft, modification, or deletion.

API: net share enum
Introduction: 3. Denial of service: An attacker could potentially abuse NetShareEnum to overload a system with unnecessary requests, leading to a denial of service (DoS) attack that disrupts the availability of network resources.

API: net share enum
Introduction: 4. Privilege escalation: If an attacker is able to exploit a vulnerability in the NetShareEnum API, they may be able to escalate their privileges on the network, gaining greater access to sensitive information and resources.

API: net share enum
Introduction: 5. Man-in-the-middle attacks: When making API calls, there is a risk that an attacker could intercept the communication between the client and server, allowing them to tamper with the data being exchanged or steal sensitive information.

API: net share enum
Introduction: It is important to properly secure and monitor API calls like NetShareEnum to prevent these security risks and protect the integrity and confidentiality of network resources.

API: kernel enumerate key
Introduction: 1. Access Control: NtEnumerateKey allows a caller to enumerate all subkeys of a specified key in the Windows registry. API: kernel enumerate key
Introduction: 2. Privilege Escalation: NtEnumerateKey may allow an attacker to escalate their privileges by manipulating the registry keys or values in a way that grants them elevated privileges. This could lead to further exploitation of the system and compromise of sensitive data.

Introduction: Overall, it is important to carefully review and validate the access control permissions and permissions checks in place when using the NtEnumerateKey API to mitigate these security risks.

API: get interface info
Introduction: 4. Injection attacks: If input to the GetInterfaceInfo API is not properly validated and sanitized, it could potentially be vulnerable to injection attacks. An attacker could manipulate the input parameters to the API in order to execute malicious code, access unauthorized network resources, or perform other unauthorized actions on the system.

API: get interface info
Introduction: 5. Server-side request forgery: If the GetInterfaceInfo API is used to make requests to external resources or systems, it could potentially be vulnerable to server-side request forgery attacks. An attacker could manipulate the input parameters to the API in order to make unauthorized requests to internal systems or resources, potentially compromising sensitive information or gaining unauthorized access to internal networks.

API: get interface info
Introduction: Overall, it is important to carefully consider the potential security risks associated with calling the GetInterfaceInfo API and ensure that proper security controls, such as input validation and access controls, are in place to mitigate these risks.

API: accept
Introduction: There are several security risks associated with calling the Windows system API "accept." Some of these risks include:

API: accept
Introduction: 1. Denial of Service (DoS) attacks: By flooding the accept function with a large number of malicious connection requests, attackers can overwhelm the system and prevent legitimate users from connecting to the server.

API: accept
Introduction: 2. Buffer overflow attacks: If the accept function is not properly bound-checked, attackers can send a specially crafted request that exceeds the size of the buffer allocated for storing network data. This can lead to a buffer overflow vulnerability, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary code on the system.

API: accept
Introduction: 3. Man-in-the-middle attacks: Attackers can intercept and manipulate the network traffic passing through the server by taking advantage of vulnerabilities in the accept function. This can allow them to eavesdrop on sensitive information, inject malicious content, or impersonate legitimate users.

API: accept
Introduction: 4. DoS attacks related to file descriptor exhaustion: The accept function creates a new file descriptor for each incoming connection request. Attackers can exhaust the system's file descriptor limit by repeatedly calling the accept function with malicious connections, causing the server to crash or become unresponsive.

API: accept
Introduction: 5. Authentication bypass: If the accept function does not properly verify the identity of the connecting clients, attackers can impersonate legitimate users and gain unauthorized access to the server resources.

API: accept
Introduction: To mitigate these security risks, developers should ensure that the accept function is properly implemented, performs input validation, error handling, and authentication checks, and securely manages resources such as file descriptors. Additionally, using secure coding practices, such as input validation, output encoding, and proper error handling, can help prevent these security risks from being exploited.

API: get system directory
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API GetSystemDirectoryA is to retrieve the path of the system directory on the Windows operating system. This function allows a program to get the path to the folder where system files, libraries, and other essential components are stored. This information can be useful for various system-level operations or when interacting with system files and libraries.

API: write console
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API WriteConsoleA is to output text to the console window in a Windows application. This API function allows developers to write characters to the console output buffer, which can be displayed on the screen for the user to see. By using WriteConsoleA, developers can easily interact with the console and provide feedback or information to the user in a text-based format.

API: kernel open file
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API NtOpenFile is to open an existing file for reading, writing, or both. This system call allows a program to access and manipulate files on the Windows system. By using NtOpenFile, developers can perform operations such as reading data from a file, writing data to a file, and changing file attributes. This API is commonly used in low-level programming tasks that require direct file access.

API: kernel create process
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API NtCreateProcessEx is to create a new process in the Windows operating system. This API allows a program to spawn a new process with specified parameters, such as the executable file path, command line arguments, and security attributes. By calling NtCreateProcessEx, a program can launch and manage a secondary process to perform specific tasks or operations, enabling more complex and flexible functionalities within the Windows environment.

API: get system info
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API GetSystemInfo is to retrieve information about the operating system and computer hardware. This API returns a SYSTEM_INFO structure containing details such as the processor architecture, number of processors, page size, memory allocation granularity, and minimum and maximum working set sizes. This information can be useful for developers in optimizing their applications for different hardware configurations and operating system versions.

API: write console
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API WriteConsoleW is to write data to the console output of a Windows application. This API allows developers to display text, characters, and other information on the console window for the user to see. By using WriteConsoleW, developers can interact with the console interface, providing feedback, displaying messages, and communicating with the user through text-based output. This API is particularly useful for command line applications, debugging tools, and text-based interfaces in Windows environments.

API: kernel read virtual memory
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API NtReadVirtualMemory is to read the contents of a specified process's virtual memory. This API function allows a program to access and read the memory of another process, enabling it to inspect or manipulate the data stored within that process's memory space. This can be useful for tasks such as debugging, monitoring, or analyzing the behavior of a running program, though it should be used carefully and responsibly to avoid causing instability or security issues.

API: remove directory
Introduction: The purpose of calling the `RemoveDirectoryA` Windows system API function is to programmatically remove a directory (folder) from the file system. This function deletes an existing directory, including all its subdirectories and files within it.

API: remove directory
Introduction: When calling `RemoveDirectoryA`, the directory must be empty before it can be successfully deleted. If the directory is not empty, the function will fail and return an error.

API: remove directory
Introduction: It is important to note that calling this API function may permanently delete files and directories from the file system, so caution should be taken when using it.

API: get key state
Introduction: The purpose of calling the GetKeyState function in the Windows system API is to retrieve the status of a specific key on the keyboard. This function allows you to determine if a key is currently pressed or not, enabling you to perform actions based on the key's status within your application or program. This can be useful for implementing shortcut keys, keyboard input handling, or any other functionality that relies on user input from the keyboard.

API: find first file
Introduction: The purpose of calling the FindFirstFileExA Windows system API is to search for files in a directory that match a specified file name or wildcard pattern. This API function allows the programmer to find the first file that matches the specified criteria and obtain information such as the file name, size, and attributes. This can be useful for file management tasks, such as finding all files with a particular extension or searching for a specific file within a directory.

API: kernel query key
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API function NtQueryKey is to query information about a specified registry key. This function allows a program to retrieve various information about a registry key, such as its name, class, last write time, and security descriptor. This information can be useful for managing and manipulating registry keys in an application.

API: open service
Introduction: The purpose of calling the OpenServiceW function in the Windows system API is to open a handle to a service specified by its service name. This function allows a program to access and interact with the specified service, such as starting, stopping, or querying its status.

API: enum windows
Introduction: The purpose of calling the EnumWindows function in the Windows system API is to retrieve information about all top-level windows currently running in the system. This function enumerates through all top-level windows, calling a specified callback function for each window found. This can be useful for tasks such as window management, automation, and monitoring of window activity in the system.

API: virtual protect
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API VirtualProtectEx is to change the protection attributes of a region of memory in a process. This can include changing the memory region to be read-only, read-write, or execute permissions. This API is commonly used in malware analysis and reverse engineering to manipulate the memory of a process. It can also be used in legitimate applications for memory protection and security purposes.

API: get volume name for volume mount point
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API GetVolumeNameForVolumeMountPointW is to retrieve the unique volume name associated with a specified volume mount point. This information can be useful for identifying and working with specific volumes on a system, such as for managing storage or performing file operations.

API: http open request
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API `HttpOpenRequestA` is to open a request for sending an HTTP request to a server, typically used for making HTTP requests to a remote server for various operations such as downloading files, sending data, or updating information. This API is part of the WinINet library in Windows, which provides functions for accessing internet resources. The `HttpOpenRequestA` function is used to open a connection to a specified resource on the internet and create an HTTP request handle that can be used to send and receive data.

API: http send request
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API HttpSendRequestA is to send an HTTP request to a server in an application using the WinINet library. This API is commonly used in Windows applications to interact with web servers and retrieve data over the internet. By using HttpSendRequestA, developers can create HTTP requests, specify headers, send data to the server, and receive responses, enabling communication between the client and the server.

API: get user name
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API GetUserNameA is to retrieve the username of the currently logged-in user on the system. This function allows a program to identify and access information specific to the user, such as user preferences, files, and permissions. This information can be used for various purposes, such as customization, authentication, and logging user activity.

API: rtl remove vectored exception handler
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API RtlRemoveVectoredExceptionHandler is to remove a previously installed vectored exception handler from the vector chain. This API can be used to deregister a previously added exception handler that was registered using the RtlAddVectoredExceptionHandler API. By calling RtlRemoveVectoredExceptionHandler, the specified exception handler is no longer called when an exception occurs. This can be useful for managing exception handling in applications and ensuring that only the necessary exception handlers are active at any given time.

API: http open request
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API HttpOpenRequestW is to create a HTTP request handle for a particular HTTP session. This API allows the application to specify the type of HTTP request (e.g. GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and other parameters such as headers and data to be sent in the request. This API is typically used in network programming to send HTTP requests to a server and receive responses.

API: http send request
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API HttpSendRequestW is to send an HTTP request to a server. This API is typically used in applications that need to communicate with web servers over HTTP, such as web browsers, download managers, or other network-related applications. By using this API, developers can send various types of HTTP requests, such as GET, POST, or PUT, and include headers and data in the request. This API is part of the WinINet library in Windows and provides a low-level interface for handling HTTP requests.

API: get system directory
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API GetSystemDirectoryW is to retrieve the path of the system directory on the Windows operating system. This function returns the path where system files, such as system libraries and executables, are stored. Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API LookupPrivilegeValueW is to retrieve the LUID (Locally Unique Identifier) for a specified privilege name on a specified system. It is commonly used in programming scenarios when working with security-related operations on Windows systems.

It provides a precise and reliable way to track time intervals on the Windows operating system.

Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API GetDiskFreeSpaceExW is to retrieve information about the amount of free space available on a disk. This API function provides detailed information such as the total size of the disk, the amount of free space, and the size of each cluster on the disk. This information can be useful for applications that need to manage disk space or monitor disk usage.

API: create remote thread
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API function `CreateRemoteThread` is to create a thread in the address space of a different process. This can be useful for various tasks such as injecting code into another process, performing remote procedure calls, or executing code in the context of a different process. This API function allows for inter-process communication and manipulation in the Windows operating system.

API: module32 first
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Module32FirstW function in the Windows system API is to retrieve information about the first module in a specified process. This can be useful for tasks such as debugging, monitoring system resources, or analyzing the memory usage of a process.

API: net share enum
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API NetShareEnum is to retrieve information about shared resources (such as folders or printers) on a network that are accessible by the current user. This API can be used to enumerate all shared resources on a remote server or on the local computer. Information returned by this API includes the names of shared resources, their type, and various attributes such as permissions and number of connections. This API can be useful for applications that need to access or manipulate shared resources on a network.

API: kernel enumerate key

Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API NtFreeVirtualMemory is to release previously allocated virtual memory in a process. This function allows a program to free up memory that is no longer needed, reducing memory usage and optimizing the performance of the system. By calling NtFreeVirtualMemory, a program can effectively manage its memory resources and prevent memory leaks.

API: reg query info key
Introduction: The purpose of calling the RegQueryInfoKeyW function in the Windows system API is to retrieve information about a specified registry key, such as the number of subkeys, the maximum length of the subkey names, the number of values, and the maximum length of the value names. This function can be helpful for developers needing to gather information about the contents of a particular registry key in order to perform operations such as enumeration or validation.

This information can be useful for querying and managing the Windows registry programmatically.

API: get system time as file time
Introduction:  is to retrieve the current system date and time as a FILETIME structure, which represents the number of 100-nanosecond intervals since January 1, 1601. OleInitialize initializes the COM library, creates a single-threaded apartment for the current thread, and initializes other system resources required for COM object communication and interoperability.

The CryptAcquireContextA function provides various options for specifying the desired CSP, container name, provider type, and flags for the cryptographic context.

API: rtl add vectored exception handler
Introduction: The purpose of calling the Windows system API RtlAddVectoredExceptionHandler is to register a Vectored Exception Handler in the process. This handler will be called for all exceptions that occur in the process, before the normal exception handling mechanism takes place. This allows developers to have more control over how exceptions are handled in their application. It can be used to catch and handle certain exceptions before they are passed to the default exception handler, or to perform additional logging or clean-up operations when an exception occurs.

API: select

This function allows a program to efficiently wait for multiple I/O operations to become ready without blocking the entire application. It is commonly used in network programming for asynchronous I/O operations.
